source_paper_title: Stochastic Games with General Payoff Functions
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Flesch_2024, title={Stochastic Games with General Payoff Functions}, volume={49}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2023.1385}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2023.1385}, number={3}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Flesch, János and Solan, Eilon}, year={2024}, month=aug, pages={1349–1371} }
context:
Page 2, Introduction (end of item [4] discussion)

Our result connects with and gives a very partial answer to the long-standing problem of whether every multiplayer stochastic game with finite action spaces, finite or countably infinite state space, and bounded and Borel-measurable payoffs admits an \(\varepsilon\)-equilibrium for every \(\varepsilon>0\).
title: Existence of epsilon-equilibria in multiplayer stochastic games with Borel payoffs
background_latex:
A (multiplayer) stochastic game is a tuple \(\Gamma=(I,S,(A_i)_{i\in I},p,(f_i)_{i\in I})\) where:
1) \(I\) is a finite nonempty set of players.
2) \(S\) is a finite or countably infinite set of states.
3) For each \(i\in I\), \(A_i\) is a finite nonempty action set; write \(A:=\prod_{i\in I} A_i\).
4) \(p:S\times A\to \Delta(S)\) is a transition kernel; when the current state is \(s\in S\) and action profile \(a\in A\) is played, the next state \(s'\in S\) is drawn according to \(p(\cdot\mid s,a)\).
5) A run (infinite play) is an infinite sequence \(r=(s_1,a_1,s_2,a_2,\dots)\in (S\times A)^\infty\) consistent with \(p\); let \(R\) denote the set of all runs. Each \(f_i:R\to\mathbb{R}\) is bounded and Borel-measurable (with respect to the product topology on \((S\times A)^\infty\) and the induced Borel \(\sigma\)-algebra on \(R\)).

A (behavior) strategy for player \(i\) is a mapping \(\sigma_i:H\to\Delta(A_i)\), where \(H\) is the set of all finite histories \((s_1,a_1,\dots,s_n)\) consistent with \(p\). A strategy profile is \(\sigma=(\sigma_i)_{i\in I}\). For each initial state \(s\in S\) and strategy profile \(\sigma\), the induced probability measure on runs is denoted \(\mathbb{P}_{s,\sigma}\) and expectation \(\mathbb{E}_{s,\sigma}\).

For \(\varepsilon\ge 0\), a strategy profile \(\sigma^*\) is an \(\varepsilon\)-equilibrium (Nash equilibrium with additive \(\varepsilon\)-slack) if for every initial state \(s\in S\), every player \(i\in I\), and every alternative strategy \(\sigma_i\),
\[\mathbb{E}_{s,\sigma^*}[f_i]\ge \mathbb{E}_{s,(\sigma_i,\sigma^*_{-i})}[f_i]-\varepsilon.\]
Equivalently, no player can improve expected payoff by more than \(\varepsilon\) via a unilateral deviation, from any specified initial state.
problem_definition_latex:
Determine whether the following statement holds:

For every stochastic game \(\Gamma=(I,S,(A_i)_{i\in I},p,(f_i)_{i\in I})\) with finite action sets \((A_i)_{i\in I}\), finite or countably infinite state space \(S\), and bounded Borel-measurable payoff functions \((f_i)_{i\in I}\), and for every \(\varepsilon>0\), there exists a strategy profile \(\sigma^*\) that is an \(\varepsilon\)-equilibrium for every initial state \(s\in S\); that is,
\[\forall s\in S,\ \forall i\in I,\ \forall \sigma_i,\quad \mathbb{E}_{s,\sigma^*}[f_i]\ge \mathbb{E}_{s,(\sigma_i,\sigma^*_{-i})}[f_i]-\varepsilon.\]
latex:

1) \(I\) is a finite nonempty set of players.
2) \(S\) is a finite or countably infinite set of states.
3) For each \(i\in I\), \(A_i\) is a finite nonempty action set; write \(A:=\prod_{i\in I} A_i\).
4) \(p:S\times A\to \Delta(S)\) is a transition kernel; when the current state is \(s\in S\) and action profile \(a\in A\) is played, the next state \(s'\in S\) is drawn according to \(p(\cdot\mid s,a)\).

Each \(f_i:R\to\mathbb{R}\) is bounded and Borel-measurable (with respect to the product topology on \((S\times A)^\infty\) and the induced Borel \(\sigma\)-algebra on \(R\)).

A (behavior) strategy for player \(i\) is a mapping \(\sigma_i:H\to\Delta(A_i)\), where \(H\) is the set of all finite histories \((s_1,a_1,\dots,s_n)\) consistent with \(p\). A strategy profile is \(\sigma=(\sigma_i)_{i\in I}\). For each initial state \(s\in S\) and strategy profile \(\sigma\), the induced probability measure on runs is denoted \(\mathbb{P}_{s,\sigma}\) and expectation \(\mathbb{E}_{s,\sigma}\).

For \(\varepsilon\ge 0\), a strategy profile \(\sigma^*\) is an \(\varepsilon\)-equilibrium (Nash equilibrium with additive \(\varepsilon\)-slack) if for every initial state \(s\in S\), every player \(i\in I\), and every alternative strategy \(\sigma_i\),

Determine whether the following statement holds:

For every stochastic game \(\Gamma=(I,S,(A_i)_{i\in I},p,(f_i)_{i\in I})\) with finite action sets \((A_i)_{i\in I}\), finite or countably infinite state space \(S\), and bounded Borel-measurable payoff functions \((f_i)_{i\in I}\), and for every \(\varepsilon>0\), there exists a strategy profile \(\sigma^*\) that is an \(\varepsilon\)-equilibrium for every initial state \(s\in S\); that is,
\[\forall s\in S,\ \forall i\in I,\ \forall \sigma_i,\quad \mathbb{E}_{s,\sigma^*}[f_i]\ge \mathbb{E}_{s,(\sigma_i,\sigma^*_{-i})}[f_i]-\varepsilon.\]
open_problem_quote: Our result connects with and gives a very partial answer to the long-standing problem of whether every multiplayer stochastic game with finite action spaces, finite or countably infinite state space, and bounded and Borel-measurable payoffs admits an \(\varepsilon\)-equilibrium for every \(\varepsilon>0\).
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The problem is a universal existence question over all bounded Borel-measurable payoff functions on infinite plays, so there is no clear finite parametrized instance family to exhaustively search. While one can search for counterexamples within restricted subclasses, failure to find one provides little evidence for the general statement.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: An evolutionary search could explore structured families of stochastic games and payoff specifications to hunt for counterexamples or to test candidate equilibrium-construction templates via approximate best-response computations. However, the full Borel-measurable generality and need for rigorous existence proofs limit the faithfulness of any computable fitness proxy.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
The open problem in Flesch--Solan (2024) asks whether every multiplayer stochastic game with finite actions, finite or countable state space, and bounded Borel payoff functions admits an \(\varepsilon\)-equilibrium that works for every initial state. The paper itself highlights that while Martin/Maitra--Sudderth style “Martin functions” can be generalized to the multiplayer setting, the resulting existence theorems stop short of full Nash existence: they yield (i) subgame \(\varepsilon\)-maxmin strategies for each player, (ii) minmax \(\varepsilon\)-acceptable profiles (individual rationality in every subgame), (iii) extensive-form correlated \(\varepsilon\)-equilibria, and (iv) existence of an \(\varepsilon\)-equilibrium in some subgame (an \(\varepsilon\)-solvable subgame), but not an \(\varepsilon\)-equilibrium simultaneously for all initial states.

Forward-citing work provides partial progress in restricted models. “Equilibrium in two-player stochastic games with shift-invariant payoffs” extends the Martin-function approach to obtain \(\varepsilon\)-equilibria for all initial states in two-player finite-state games under the additional structural assumption of shift-invariant (prefix-independent) Borel payoffs, adapting Vieille-style reductions and using determinacy and deviation-detection ideas. On the multiplayer side, “Regularity of the minmax value and equilibria in multiplayer Blackwell games” develops regularity/approximation results for minmax values with Borel (or upper semi-analytic) payoffs and proves \(\varepsilon\)-equilibrium existence in multiplayer Blackwell games under strong hypotheses such as history-independent minmax values; these results suggest that controlling the history dependence of \(v_i(h)\) is a key obstruction.

Several papers contribute tools rather than full existence theorems. “Identifying the deviator” supplies a general Borel-measurable deviation-identification mechanism that underpins detection-and-punishment constructions, which are needed in Flesch--Solan’s \(\varepsilon\)-solvable subgame result and in related equilibrium proofs for tail/shift-invariant objectives. Classical determinacy results (“Borel games” and determinacy for constrained Blackwell games) remain foundational for the zero-sum components and approximation arguments but do not by themselves resolve multiplayer Nash existence with Nature. Overall, the general statement remains open; current techniques either require two players, additional payoff structure (tail/shift invariance), or extra regularity assumptions ensuring stable minmax behavior across subgames.

sources.title: Equilibrium in two-player stochastic games with shift-invariant payoffs
sources.theorem_or_result: The paper proves: every two-player stochastic game with finite state and action sets and bounded, Borel-measurable, shift-invariant payoff functions admits an \(\varepsilon\)-equilibrium for every \(\varepsilon>0\) (Theorem 2.20).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for existence of \(\varepsilon\)-equilibria in multiplayer stochastic games with bounded Borel payoffs (finite actions; finite or countable states), uniformly for all initial states. This paper addresses a significant special case: it establishes existence for two-player (nonzero-sum) stochastic games with finite state/action sets under the additional structural assumption that payoffs are shift-invariant (prefix-independent). It does not treat (i) more than two players, (ii) general Borel payoffs without shift-invariance, or (iii) countably infinite state spaces; the authors explicitly note the countable-state extension is open and discuss obstacles. Thus it provides partial progress and new techniques (e.g., deviation detection using inner regularity and Lévy’s zero-one law) that may be relevant for broader cases.

sources.title: Regularity of the minmax value and equilibria in multiplayer Blackwell games
sources.bibtex:
@article{AshkenaziGolanEtAl_RegularityMinmaxBlackwell_2024,
  title        = {Regularity of the minmax value and equilibria in multiplayer Blackwell games},
  author       = {Ashkenazi-Golan, Yair and others},
  year         = {2024},
  doi          = {10.1007/s11856-024-2679-9},
  journal      = {Israel Journal of Mathematics},
  note         = {DOI provided in forward-citation analysis; complete bibliographic fields may be added from the journal record},
}
sources.theorem_or_result: The paper proves existence of \(\varepsilon\)-equilibria for certain classes of multiplayer Blackwell games (simultaneous-move, no Nature) with general measurable payoffs. In particular (Theorem 4.3): if each player has finite action sets at every history, payoffs \(f_i\) are bounded and upper semi-analytic, and each player’s minmax value \(v_i(f_i)\) is history-independent across subgames, then for every \(\varepsilon>0\) the game admits an \(\varepsilon\)-equilibrium. It also proves (Theorem 3.1) regularity of the minmax for Borel winning sets: \(v_i(W)=\sup\{v_i(C): C\subseteq W\text{ closed}\}=\inf\{v_i(O): O\supseteq W\text{ open}\}\), and (Theorem 3.3) approximation of \(v_i(f)\) for bounded Borel \(f\) by bounded limsup functions from below/above.
sources.connection: The open problem asks for \(\varepsilon\)-equilibria in *multiplayer stochastic games with Nature* (state space finite/countable, finite actions) for *arbitrary bounded Borel payoffs*, uniformly for every initial state. This paper works in the Blackwell-game model (simultaneous moves on a game tree) without an explicit stochastic transition kernel; it proves \(\varepsilon\)-equilibria only under additional structural assumptions—most notably history-independence of each player’s minmax value (Theorem 4.3), or a strong condition on analytic winning sets plus \(\sum_i v_i(W_i)>n-1\) (Theorem 4.1). Thus it does not resolve the general existence question for all Borel payoffs in stochastic games. However, it contributes relevant tools (regularity/approximation of minmax) and yields existence for important subclasses (e.g., tail-measurable payoffs imply history-independent minmax, giving \(\varepsilon\)-equilibria in that subclass). The authors explicitly list extending these methods to stochastic games with Nature as an open direction.

sources.title: Identifying the deviator
sources.bibtex:
@article{AlonEtAl_IdentifyingDeviator_2024,
  title        = {Identifying the deviator},
  author       = {Alon, Noga and others},
  year         = {2024},
  doi          = {10.1214/24-AAP2077.short},
  journal      = {The Annals of Applied Probability},
  note         = {DOI provided in forward-citation analysis; complete bibliographic fields may be added from the journal record},
}
sources.theorem_or_result: Main general result: For any finite player set with finite action sets, any prescribed behavior-strategy profile \(\sigma^*\) and any Borel target set \(D\subseteq A^{\mathbb N}\) with \(\mathbb P_{\sigma^*}(D)=1\), there exists a Borel blame function \(f:D^c\to I\) such that for every player \(i\) and every unilateral deviation strategy \(\sigma_i\), \(\mathbb P_{\sigma_i,\sigma^*_{-i}}(D^c\cap\{f\neq i\})=0\) (Theorem 2.9). More generally, if \(\mathbb P_{\sigma^*}(D)>1-\varepsilon\), then \((\sigma^*,D)\) is \(\delta\)-testable with \(\delta\le 2\sqrt{(|I|-1)\varepsilon}\) (Theorem 2.6).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for existence of \(\varepsilon\)-equilibria in multiplayer stochastic games with countable states and bounded Borel payoffs, uniformly for every initial state. This paper does not construct equilibria or prove existence in that generality. Instead it proves a deviation-identification ("blame") theorem for repeated-action processes: whenever a prescribed strategy profile \(\sigma^*\) achieves a Borel target event \(D\) with probability 1 (or high probability), there is a Borel rule that, upon observing failure of \(D\), identifies a unique deviator with zero (or small) error probability against any unilateral deviation. Such identification is a key ingredient in equilibrium constructions based on detection-and-punishment. The authors explicitly note applications: an alternative proof of \(\varepsilon\)-equilibrium existence in repeated games with tail-measurable payoffs, and (citing Flesch–Solan 2023b) existence of an \(\varepsilon\)-equilibrium in some subgame for finite-state stochastic games with bounded Borel payoffs. However, the paper itself does not resolve the full open problem (notably it does not address countably infinite state spaces, nor guarantee an \(\varepsilon\)-equilibrium for every initial state in the original game).

sources.title: Borel games
sources.theorem_or_result: Main theorem (Martin 1975): every two-player perfect-information infinite game with a Borel winning set is determined; i.e., for any Borel set \(A\subseteq \mathbb{N}^\omega\) (equivalently Borel subset of the play space), one of the two players has a winning strategy in the corresponding Gale–Stewart game \(G(A)\).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for existence of \(\varepsilon\)-Nash equilibria in multiplayer stochastic games with bounded Borel-measurable payoffs. Martin’s Borel determinacy concerns zero-sum, two-player, perfect-information win/lose games (no stochastic transitions, no simultaneous moves, no multi-player Nash equilibrium). It does not directly imply \(\varepsilon\)-equilibria in general stochastic games, but it is a foundational descriptive-set-theoretic result often used to justify measurability/selection and value/determinacy statements for Borel objectives in certain two-player (especially turn-based) game models, which can be ingredients in equilibrium-existence proofs for restricted classes (e.g., zero-sum or perfect-information settings).

sources.title: The Determinacy of Blackwell Games with a Constraint Function
sources.theorem_or_result: The paper proves determinacy for a class of two-player zero-sum Blackwell games (simultaneous moves, perfect monitoring) with Borel payoff of restricted complexity: if the payoff is the indicator of a \,\(G_{\delta\sigma}\) set \(S\subseteq (X\times Y)^\mathbb{N}\) (with \(X,Y\) finite), then the Blackwell game \(\Gamma(S)\) is determined (Theorem 3.10). It also proves determinacy for open sets (Corollary 3.2), \(G_\delta\) sets (Corollary 3.9), and continuous payoff functions (Corollary 3.4), and develops a truncation/approximation method via nested equivalent truncated subgames (Section 2.3, Theorem 3.1, Theorem 3.7).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for existence of \(\varepsilon\)-Nash equilibria in general multiplayer stochastic games with countable states and bounded Borel payoffs. This paper addresses a different but adjacent existence question: \(\varepsilon\)-determinacy (value existence) in two-player zero-sum simultaneous-move infinite games (Blackwell games) for certain Borel payoff classes (up to \(G_{\delta\sigma}\), plus continuous). Determinacy in such zero-sum games is related in spirit to equilibrium existence, and the truncation/nested-subgame approximation techniques could be relevant tools for proving \(\varepsilon\)-equilibria in stochastic games. However, it does not treat multiplayer nonzero-sum Nash equilibria, does not handle general Borel payoffs (explicitly states that case remains open for Blackwell games), and does not incorporate a state-based stochastic transition kernel as in stochastic games.
